data.title: The Utterly Tranquil Feeling Of Watching Your Passive Income Grow
data.description:
I wasn't born as an investor.

Nor did I grow up with an entrepreneurial spirit and drive to make as much money as possible. I never opened a lemonade stand as a kid, and I always dreaded having to sell candy bars door to door for my Little League team (where that money went I'll never know).

In college, I majored in film, but intellectually I was more drawn to philosophy, theology, and the liberal arts more broadly.

My goal, in retrospect, seemed to be to become the smartest unemployable person I could be.

Fortunately, post-college, I had opportunities to become more, shall we say, commercially valuable -- first working in residential real estate, and then moving into commercial real estate.

My first big investment, initiated by the prodding of my boss/mentor, was a rental property -- a duplex, to be precise. The experience of being a landlord taught me plenty about investing, but it revealed even more about myself.

"Knowing yourself is the beginning of all wisdom," in the ancient words of Aristotle.

The building itself had been built in the early 1980s and had various maintenance issues and repair needs, none of which my soft hands or liberal arts brain knew how to fix. That meant constantly relying on the paid labor of a handyman and other occasional contractors. During those blessed streaks in which nothing broke, cash flow after the mortgage payment would slowly accrue. And then the annual property tax bill would come and wipe out all that accrued cash.

The only return I saw on that rental property investment was the price appreciation realized after about 6 years of ownership -- not including, of course, all the time and money spent maintaining it.

That formative experience of landlording may have been a poor monetary investment, but at the same time, it did make me into an investor. By experiencing a form of investing that didn't work for me, it made me want to find a form that would work.

It taught me three valuable lessons about investing and especially about myself:

  1. I like cash flow. Free cash flow. The kind I can actually consider personal income. The ultimate goal of investing is to achieve recurring cash returns. That is true at both the company level (cash profits) and the shareholder level (dividends).
  2. I like passive income. The kind that doesn't require me to spend money (and/or time) to make money. The kind with no strings attached.
  3. I don't like the types of investments where the entire return comes from selling the asset.

You can probably see why the landlord life wasn't for me, and why the path of dividend growth is the one I tread today.

It should also be clear from Point #3 why I don't invest in stocks that don't pay a dividend. I want ongoing cash returns, not just the speculation (however well informed) of future gains. And I want to personally compound my asset ownership through reinvesting the dividends paid out of those cash returns.

Being a landlord gave me no tranquility. Neither has the handful of ultra-low-yielding or non-dividend-paying investments I've owned over the years.

But owning a portfolio of dividend growth stocks that pay growing dividends that I can selectively and opportunistically reinvest to further grow my passive income?

Now that is utter tranquility for me.

My portfolio income has been large enough to be worth tracking for the last four years or so, and tracking it has been an act of pure joy.

Although there have been a few relatively shallow dips -- mainly from the W. P. Carey (WPC) dividend cut in late 2023 -- the overall trajectory of my quarterly passive income has been steadily upward:

In Q3 2025, my total portfolio income grew 12.2% year-over-year and 7.1% quarter-over-quarter.

Mind you, this is from dividend growth, reinvested dividends, the occasional portfolio recycling, and monthly infusions of surplus labor income.

By my rough estimate, my ~5%-yielding portfolio's average dividend growth rate is about 4-5% annually. Add in another 4-5% from reinvesting all dividends as well as another 1-2% from investing surplus labor income, and my total portfolio income growth should exceed 10% per year.

(Assuming no substantial dividend cuts, of course.)

Dividend growth investing can feel painfully slow, as it takes a while for compounding to really take off. Dividends can be so small in the beginning -- measured in pennies instead of dollars. But with enough time, 10% annual growth in portfolio income eventually becomes a powerful force.

At some point, your reinvested dividends exceed your contributions from surplus labor income.

For a fortunate group of DGIers, total portfolio income eventually exceeds total labor income.

Although there are many different definitions of "financial freedom," one prominent one is surely the point at which one's passive income exceeds one's labor income.

To be fair, it does feel good to see stock price appreciation in one's portfolio. But for the hedonic treadmill within the human brain, unrealized gains quickly become the default, the norm, the expected.

Dividends, however, provide a regular double-dopamine hit from (1) the infusion of cash in one's account and (2) the ability to selectively reinvest that cash into more shares.

So where in today's expensive market does a DGIer like me selectively and opportunistically invest?

Let's zoom out.

Looking For Where To Invest In A Dangerously Expensive Market

I casually said above that the stock market is expensive. That's admittedly a subjective statement.

Here's a more objective statement: the forward price-to-earnings ratio of the S&P 500 (SPY), which is heavily tilted toward Big Tech, has not been this high since the dot-com bubble of the late 1990s.

Yes, Big Tech stocks typically have high profit margins, which justify a higher earnings multiple for the market.

For example, the software giants Meta Platforms (META), Microsoft (MSFT), and Alphabet (GOOG, GOOGL) all have profit margins in the 30-40% range, which seems to defy the capitalistic principle that competition will drive down high margins.

It appears that the competitive advantages of these tech conglomerates are, for now, able to sustain their high margins. And that matters, because Big Tech makes up about 1/3rd of the S&P 500's market capitalization. These three corporations alone account for over 14% of the index.

Even so, when the market has reached these heights of valuation in the past, it has translated into poor returns over the next five years.

In fact, according to the chart above, there have been no historical points in time when the market sported its current valuation that generated a positive total return in the subsequent 5-year period.

Let that sink in.

It might be different this time.

But if history is any guide, investors should think twice before investing in the S&P 500 or another broadly diversified fund or ETF.

Of course, there are some things that are different this time. As many have pointed out already, the buildout of AI data centers today is largely being done by companies with huge cash flows. These are large, highly profitable companies, and therefore their valuations, while potentially rich, are nowhere near the levels seen during the dot-com bubble.

In the late 1990s, many tech companies with little to no earnings and sketchy business plans enjoyed rapid appreciation in stock prices.

But another difference is that private equity and private credit are playing a much larger role in financing the data center buildout than they did 25 years ago in financing the Internet infrastructure buildout.

The unprofitable private company OpenAI has raised hundreds of billions of dollars in equity and debt capital as well as publicly traded tech giants, and it is arguably the ringleader of circular financing network fueling AI infrastructure capex.

If OpenAI were publicly traded, it would surely cause the tech sector's overall valuation to rise considerably, because OpenAI has a very high "P" but zero "E."

In other words, the price of its equity is high, but the company generates no earnings.

As discussed in "If AI Is A Bubble, The Signs Were Staring Us In The Face," this circular flow of spending within the AI-tech ecosystem assumes that commercial applications of AI will grow sufficiently quickly and be sufficiently profitable to make this capex worthwhile.

And there is quite a lot of AI capex going on. While Fed rate hikes and a slowing economy have pulled down all other forms of private fixed investment, investment in "information processing equipment" (here a euphemism for AI capex) continues to surge ahead at a pace of 20% year-over-year.

I am sure demand for profitable applications of AI will continue to grow, but the question is whether they will grow at the same speed that AI capex is growing.

If, at some point, investors realize that profitable demand (not just demand for free versions of AI that can scarcely be monetized) will grow slower than initially expected, it could burst the AI bubble.

To quote Jonathan Weil from the Wall Street Journal:

Circularity can be virtuous on the upside, and vicious on the downside. The deals can work fine, until they don’t.

That is why I label the current market "dangerously expensive."

It might be more than just run-of-the-mill overvalued. It might be overvalued based on artificial earnings from the AI ecosystem's huge circular financing deals.

That would make the market "double-overvalued" -- a high valuation multiple based on inflated, fragile, and contrived earnings.

So where should one look to invest instead of the AI-tech space?

The energy sector (XLE) is a relatively cheap and high-yielding option. The XLE ETF yields ~3.4%, and the sector sports a forward P/E ratio of about 15x compared to the S&P 500's ~23x.

But the problem with the energy sector, or at least oil and gas producers, is that there appears to be no shortage of global oil and gas supplies.

US production has boomed in recent years, adding ample supply to the global market, and meanwhile there is expected to be only meager growth in demand over the next year or so.

This should continue to put downward pressure on the price of oil, which should in turn pressure the fundamentals of oil & gas producers.

What about the healthcare sector (XLV)?

The healthcare sector's forward P/E ratio of 17.5x is in a bit of a dip right now as GLP-1 drugs change the drug demand landscape and a pharma-skeptical HHS secretary steadily chips away at the credibility of drugmakers.

On the other hand, as developed nations' populations continue to age, demand for healthcare products and services should continue to increase overall.

That makes me, as a DGIer, particularly interested in dividend-paying healthcare stocks right now.

My Buy List This Week

That seems like the right time to shift over to my buy list, which currently has two individual stocks and three DGI ETFs on it.

STOCKS Dividend Yield Projected Dividend Growth Rate (Guesstimate)
American Tower (AMT) 3.6% Mid- to High-Single-Digit
Hannon Armstrong Sustainable Infrastructure (HASI) 6.0% Mid-Single-Digit
ETFs
VictoryShares US Large Cap High Div Vol-Weighted ETF (CDL) 3.4% Mid-Single-Digit
Global X MLP & Energy Infrastructure ETF (MLPX) 4.9% Mid-Single-Digit
TBG Dividend Focus ETF (TBG) 3.7% Mid-Single-Digit

American Tower

AMT is a name I've talked a lot about in recent months. It's a wireless infrastructure REIT that owns about 150,000 cell towers across the world, mostly in developed nations, and should benefit from steady organic growth as mobile data usage continues to surge.

Hannon Armstrong Sustainable Infrastructure

HASI is a financier of green energy and decarbonization projects that I believe will benefit from the huge need to build power production facilities to support data centers. If anything, the loss of renewable energy tax credits will widen the opportunity for HASI as a creative financier of deals.

The VictoryShares ETF

CDL is a very unique dividend growth ETF wherein holdings are weighted by both dividend yield and volatility, rewarding lower volatility stocks with a larger weight in the portfolio. That effectively loads CDL full of utilities, which is its largest sectoral weighting at 26%.

The combination of a >3% yield and >7% compound annual dividend growth over the last decade makes CDL an excellent choice for DGIers.

The Midstream Energy ETF

In the discussion on the energy sector above, I specified that oil & gas producers should continue to be pressured by the slight oversupply of oil and gas. But that doesn't diminish the volumetric throughput of oil and gas for midstream assets like pipelines, storage facilities, petrochemical plants, and export terminals.

The high-quality North American midstream companies in MLPX operate today with fairly lean balance sheets, healthy payout ratios, and ample growth opportunities thanks to the power needs of AI data centers.

TBG Dividend Focus ETF

The Bahnsen Group, including the eponymous portfolio manager David Bahnsen, manage this thoroughly dividend growth oriented ETF that strikes a strong balance between moderate yield and moderately high dividend increases.

The combination of a 3.7% dividend yield and 5-7% annual dividend growth strikes me as a compelling long-term investment.

Moreover, the portfolio managers at TBG clearly see value in certain healthcare stocks, as I do, which is why healthcare is currently their highest sectoral weighting:

Not many dividend ETFs overweight healthcare to such a degree, but I believe now is the right time to do so, if there is ever a right time.

data.title: Canadian Natural Resources: Competitive Leadership And New Sanctions Create Conditions For Growth
data.description:
Investment Thesis

The recent news that has shaken the oil market opens up additional potential for strategic growth for companies involved in oil production in Canada. The Canadian Natural Resources Limited (NYSE:CNQ) is the largest producer of Canadian oil, with high financial stability and potential for further scaling of export activities. With CNQ's per-barrel production costs significantly lower than those of its main industry competitors in North America, it has the guarantee of maintaining positive financial results during a downturn in the oil market.

Moreover, new sanctions against Russia's oil industry could potentially halt Russian oil supplies to China and India. If the capacity of the Trans Mountain pipeline is fully utilized, Canadian Natural Resources could increase its oil supplies by concluding new agreements with Chinese oil refineries. Moreover, for oil prices, the new sanctions against Russian oil companies are an additional factor that, if not raising the cost of hydrocarbons, then at least halting its decline in the short and medium term.

Considering CNQ's positive outlook and competitive advantages, I recommend these shares as a "Buy." In this article, I will reveal more detailed information about the reasons behind my decision. That said, I am not an active supporter of the bullish camp when it comes to oil companies. For instance, my latest fundamental analysis of the oil company Civitas Resources (CIVI) resulted in a "Sell" recommendation. I see a similar assessment for most participants in the North American oil market, due to the previously negative impact of fundamental factors.

Canadian Natural Resources' strategy and competitive advantages

Canadian Natural Resources is Canada's largest oil producer with a diversified portfolio of assets in North America, parts of the North Sea, and the African shelf. The CNQ business strategy is aimed at creating safe, cost-effective, and environmentally responsible production activities, resulting in the strategic development of the company and growth in the value of its assets and brand. The company's management places great emphasis on achieving a high level of financial stability. The company has an impressive track record of generating adjusted cash flow, which is utilized to scale operations, improve operational processes (including reducing CO2 emissions), and pay dividends to shareholders. The natural course of action for Canadian Natural Resources in recent years has been to buy back shares, increasing the investment attractiveness of the securities for their shareholders.

The competitive advantage of the company is the diversification of its economic activities, because it has a diverse structure for the production and sale of hydrocarbons. The bulk of its product portfolio is high-quality synthetic oil (36% of the total share), while heavy oil accounts for 26% of the company's total revenue. The other 27% of the portfolio is focused on natural gas production, and the remaining 11% is on light oil and liquefied natural gas (LNG). Due to its performance, Canadian Natural Resources is the largest producer of crude oil in Canada and the 2nd largest producer of natural gas.

The record volumes of crude oil production and one of the largest volumes of natural gas production in Canada are due to another competitive advantage of Canadian Natural Resources – its proven reserves, with a life span of about 33 years (based on current energy production volumes). The Canadian oil and gas sector median is approximately 17 years. In its latest quarterly report, CNQ increased its proven and probable oil equivalent reserves by 9%. They now total approximately 20.1 billion barrels.

Potential for oil exports to China and the impact of new sanctions on Russia

On October 22, 2025, Donald Trump imposed new large-scale economic sanctions on Russia. The Russian energy sector was hit hard. It was announced that the foreign economic activities of Russia's two largest oil companies, Rosneft and Lukoil, would be completely blocked. Not only parent companies, but also all subsidiaries, numbering more than 30 entities, are subject to sanctions. The adopted measures block the companies' international financial transactions and cooperation with creditors and counterparties. Against this background, Brent crude oil prices showed a daily increase of 3.3%.

Risks for the oil sector from these sanctions began to emerge immediately. The news that India's largest oil refineries planned to stop purchasing Russian oil was followed by reports that China's state-owned refineries were terminating short-term purchase contracts with Russia. Reasons for this decision by Indian and Chinese producers are related to the risks of financial restrictions being imposed for activities and cooperation with Rosneft and Lukoil. Based on data for the second half of 2025, the average supply of crude oil from Russia to China is 2 mln barrels per day, and to India – 1.5 mln barrels per day. Most of the supplies were delivered by sea.

This news is super important for Canadian Natural Resources' strategic development because CNQ is Canada's main export-focused supplier. As Canada's leading oil producer, the opening up of new potential for hydrocarbon exports to China represents an opportunity to increase revenue. The expanded Trans Mountain (TMX) pipeline in May 2024 gave Canadian producers new access to the Pacific coast. This has opened up the possibility of maritime logistics for export operations to supply oil from Canada to Asian markets, primarily China.

Based on data from June 2024 to June 2025, the average daily volume of Canadian oil shipments to China was about 207,000 barrels. Since the beginning of October 2025, however, these exports have increased sharply, averaging 333,000 barrels of oil for the first half of the month. During the first 15 days of October, approximately 5 million barrels of Canadian oil were shipped from the seaport of Vancouver, 70% of which is redirected to the Chinese market.

Additionally, on October 23, 2025, Reuters reported that: "the operator of Canada's Trans Mountain pipeline and oil shippers are in talks to resolve a two-year-old shipping cost dispute that has deterred usage of the pipeline and added uncertainty to the government's plans to eventually sell it." The subject of the dispute is to reduce the cost of using the pipeline for Canadian companies, including CNQ. Currently, the fee for using the infrastructure is twice as high as originally expected during the planning process for the capacity expansion project (discussions took place as far back as 2017). With these fees affecting the economic viability of exporting oil to Asian markets, the outcome of the settlement will determine how much Canadian oil Canadian Natural Resources will ship to China.

Canadian Natural Resources Financial Profile

The most recent information provided in the Q2 2025 report reflects an improvement in Canadian Natural Resources' financial profile. I would first like to draw attention to the cost indicators, which are important for companies in the oil and gas industry. The data shows that CNQ's average operating costs for natural gas production are CAD 1.19 per 1,000 cubic feet. This figure is down 10% compared to Q2 2024. CNQ's average operating costs for synthetic oil and heavy oil production are CAD 26.53 and CAD 17.44 per barrel. These values changed by +2% and 0% compared to Q2 2024. Nevertheless, these figures are below the median for the sector in the Canadian and US economies.

In order to understand the difference between Canadian Natural Resources' oil production costs and the average figures in Canada and other countries around the world, refer to the following chart, which depicts the cost of producing 1 barrel of oil, expressed in US dollars.

I believe that these costs are CNQ's competitive advantage. These operating costs are low, especially when considering the characteristics of oil production from Canadian oil sands. Their physicochemical properties determine their high bitumen content, making oil production in Canada less efficient and of lower quality. The Canadian oil has to go through an enrichment stage to get a higher-quality synthetic product. It should also be noted that the capital costs of starting such production are higher than drilling traditional wells to extract the oil equivalent. Further, however, operating costs are fixed, allowing for lower break-even points.

Consequently, Canadian Natural Resources has a balanced and diversified product portfolio that includes natural gas, heavy and light crude oil, bitumen, and synthetic crude oil, complemented by a balanced and diversified marketing strategy. By selling these products at prices higher than the cost of production and the sector median, the CNQ gains an additional catalyst for strategic growth through improved financial results.

As for key financial indicators, non-GAAP earnings per share for Q2 2025 amounted to CAD 0.71. This is CAD 0.06 higher than the consensus estimate of analysts, but CAD 0.17 lower than in Q2 2024. A key reason for the decline in earnings compared to last year is low oil prices, which have been on a downward trend. Meanwhile, Canadian Natural Resources' revenue for the second quarter of 2025 fell to US$8.7 billion, down US$0.4 billion from a year earlier. While the decline in oil prices was greater than the decline in the company's revenue, this was offset by a change in the sales mix. The CNQ increased its sales of natural gas, while in price terms, sales of oil equivalent and gas condensate declined. Details of the CNQ's key financial results are shown below.

The adjusted cash flow amounted to approximately CAD 3.3 billion. The strong balance sheet is noteworthy, as Canadian Natural Resources had CAD 4.8 billion in cash as of the end of the previous quarter.

Third Quarter 2025 Earnings Report And Share Buyback

Canadian Natural Resources plans to release its Q3 2025 earnings report on November 6. Consensus estimates indicate earnings per share of $0.58. This is a 16.3% decrease from the third quarter of 2025. I think there is a high probability that the actual results will exceed the forecast, since from the last 8 quarterly reports, 5 had a positive surprise and 3 had a negative surprise.

What's important to keep an eye on are comments about potentially expanding oil exports to foreign markets, especially in Asia, considering the recent news about economic sanctions against Russian oil companies. On this basis, I suggest that CNQ management may potentially adjust its potential revenue and EPS for Q4 2025. The impact of the sanctions policy could cause oil prices to recover more quickly than analysts had previously expected. It is interesting to note that there have been 11 earnings revisions by analysts over the past three months. 7 of them have revised their EPS forecasts upwards.

Further comments from management on plans to continue share buybacks are another important point in CNQ's expected Q3 2025 earnings report. This is a key factor of interest to the company's stakeholders, since Canadian Natural Resources follows a financial policy of returning capital to shareholders. Starting in Q1 2020 through Q2 2025, the total volume of share repurchases amounted to 287 million CNQ shares. This has reduced the total supply of shares in circulation by 12.12%.

Thanks to this decision, capital is being returned, which improves a number of key metrics for the shares (e.g., EPS). Taking the information for Q2 2025, the company repurchased 8.6 million shares for a total of C$352.6 million (at an average price of CAD 41 per share). I believe that if management confirms plans to continue the share buyback in its comments, it will continue to be a positive factor in assessing CNQ's investment attractiveness. In case of the opposite, a comment on the termination of the share buyback will provoke a possible sale. However, the probability of such a decision, judging by past comments from management, is unlikely.

CNQ Stock Assessment

Assessing the growth potential of CNQ shares, I propose to refer to the technical analysis of its chart with a 1W time frame. The point is that Canadian Natural Resources securities have been in a downtrend since April 2024. The confirmation of this is the formed bearish trend channel.

At the moment, prices are trying to break above the upper border of the channel, creating conditions for a reversal of the trend to an upward one. As confirmation, earlier in May 2025, the price tested an important support level of 25.62. Having conducted a liquidity raid, a candlestick pattern formed in response, signaling a price increase. A new support level formed at 29.30, which helps buyers in their attempt to break through the upper border of the upward trend channel. According to my opinion, the technical analysis of the CNQ chart reflects a potential reversal of the long-term trend to an upward one, which would allow the stock to return to its maximum levels ($38-41). The timeframe for the implementation of such a plan could be around 6-12 months.

Also in order to evaluate CNQ, we will analyze the key metrics of the Quantitative Rating, which show that Canadian Natural Resources shares are more attractive for investment compared to the industry average. Let's first look at some of the values from the Valuation Grade and Underlying Metrics group. CNQ's overall rating is average for the market. But if we look at key price/earnings ratios, we'll see that the company's value is lower than the sector median. For instance, the P/E GAAP (TTM) is 10.5, while the median is 14.07. That opens up about 25% growth potential for the stock to reach industry averages.

Also, let's check out the Profitability Grade and Underlying Metrics. The ratios here are way better than the sector median. These indicators reflect CNQ's high investment attractiveness through its performance in achieving high levels of profitability and returns. These stocks are classified as financially sound, because the issuing company has a profitable business, stable revenues, and an adjusted cash flow. Taking into account the risks that Canadian Natural Resources has faced in the past, it is an excellent result. Now imagine what the valuation of CNQ shares will be when oil prices rise. These projections generate interest in these securities, which reflects their value to investors.

Risks Of The Investment Thesis

As my recommendation for CNQ is "Buy," risks to the investment idea are directly related to threat factors that could impact the effectiveness of Canadian Natural Resources’ business model and business strategy. As part of the company's conference call following the presentation of its Q2 2025 quarterly results, management identified the following key risks: geopolitical and trade instability around the world, threats to the stability of global supply chains, the negative impact of seasonal equipment maintenance, the negative impact of weather factors on energy production, and market risks related to oil and natural gas prices.

According to me, the key risk to my investment idea is the continuation of the downward trend in the oil market. Taking into account current factors and news that are creating barriers to Russian oil exports to the global market (especially to China and India), the conditions are ripe for a reversal of the downward trend to an upward one. However, we do not know all the possible scenarios of the "geopolitical game" that is being played in the modern world. So, if new catalysts for a drop in oil prices appear, for example, to $50 per barrel and below, this will most likely cause me to revise my position on CNQ downward. Considering the current agenda, I do not particularly believe in the likelihood of such a negative scenario. My opinion, however, may be subjective (as is the case with all of us). It is therefore worth paying close attention to the potential impact of price risk on the dynamics of CNQ's value.

Conclusion

In summary, the results of my fundamental analysis of Canadian Natural Resources Limited shares allow me to evaluate them as an asset worth adding to today's investment portfolio. The main factors include the company's competitive advantages (record reserves and low cost of energy production), high financial stability (accompanied by dividend growth and share buybacks, stable adjusted cash flow, and reliable solvency), and new geopolitical factors that create potential for a recovery in prices on the global oil market (economic sanctions against the Russian oil sector). In this case, the existing risks are limited, in fact, only to the downward trend in the oil market, which has every chance to come to an end.

data.title: How To Retire Early
data.description:
Introduction:

Not everyone wants to retire early. After all, a 9-5 day job brings steady income, a sense of purpose, and keeps one engaged in physical and mental activity. In fact, so many folks find it difficult to adjust to a retired life routine, where there is too much time and not much to do. However, we are talking of retiring early, where one has more energy and purpose, so they can find something to do that is more meaningful and interesting.

What are the prerequisites to retire early? When we say early it simply means earlier than the usual 62-65 retirement age. In fact, a lot of people (certainly, not everyone) would like to retire somewhat sooner, maybe at 55. Of course, some would like to retire at 45, but that is not practical for most folks. So, we would just consider (for this article) that aiming to retire at 55 years is both possible and practical. It also gives a person a chance to pursue things that they have always wanted to do but never had a chance with a 9-5 job. This could be pursuing a hobby that could also bring some income. With our world becoming more and more digital, such things are easily possible. For example, if someone likes to travel around the world, they could share their experiences online and turn it into an income-generating travel blog/vlog. Or one could take up a part-time job that gets you some income and health benefits while still having enough time doing other things of interest.

The question is how to get to a level where you have the confidence that you can quit your 9-5 day job and simply survive on income from your savings. This is a tall order and should be very carefully thought through.

How Can the Stock Market Help?

The broader market, as represented by the S&P 500 (SP500), has been making new all-time highs almost on a regular basis. In fact, we are in a bull market that started at the end of 2022, after the brief period of the tech crash of 2022. Will this uptrend continue, and how long? No one can answer that question reliably. We know that the S&P 500 has beaten almost everything during the last 10-12 years, and especially has outperformed the international markets. But that has started to change, and this year the European markets have been outperforming the S&P 500. Also, even during bull markets, the S&P 500 goes through deep corrections on a regular basis. On the income front, there is hardly any income that you can earn from such index funds. A fund like the S&P 500 is a good choice during the long-term accumulation phase, but not so much during the retirement or withdrawal phase. So, we will discuss the alternatives where you can not only generate a large enough income, but also the growth that is critical to sustain a long retirement. Especially when we are talking of retiring early at 55, it may mean supporting 40-plus years of retirement. To achieve these objectives, we need three types of investment buckets:

  • Income-focused bucket.
  • Growth-focused bucket.
  • A Hedging bucket to bring about low volatility and less drawdowns.

Estimation of the Retirement Savings that You Need:

We will assume that our early retiree is a 55-year-old couple. Now, how much savings capital would they require to sustain their desired lifestyle? Broadly, this is an individualistic question as the answer would vary greatly from person to person. But we can still estimate it in a manner that could work for a vast majority of middle-income folks.

We think that you should not aim to withdraw more than 6% average income from your investment portfolio. Sure, there are so many investments that can give you 8%, 10% or even 12% income, but usually that comes at the cost of growth. If you want your investments to beat the inflation year after year, 6% (or less) of income is the most you should expect.

So, we will estimate on the basis of 6% average income. If our retiree were to need $50,000 a year to sustain their expenses, at 6% average income, they must have roughly $835,000 in savings.

Investment capital = Yearly Expense / 0.06.

However, it does not mean you need $835,000 because, at some point, you will be eligible to withdraw Social Security benefits. At that point, your income needs will be less than what you estimated. The table below will make it self-explanatory. We are assuming that in the case of our retiree couple, one of the spouses will start withdrawing SS benefits at 62, while the second one will withdraw at 70 years of age. The age to be eligible for full SS benefits is 66 years and 10 months. You withdraw before, and you will get less, while if you wait until 70, you get much more. In the table below, we assume that the spouse who withdraws at 62 gets roughly $1,800 per month, while the other one who starts at 70 will get $3,600 per month.

Now, we will assume that our couple will have only $600,000 at 55, and they will have to withdraw a higher percentage of income in the first few years, but it will drop when SS benefits start coming in.

However, the early retiree should also make separate provision for additional expenses, such as medical premiums for the next 10 years (until one reaches 65 and is eligible for Medicare) and two years' worth of living expenses.

Investment Capital = $600,000

Add Savings for Med Prem. = $120,000 (10,000 per year for 10 years)

Add 2 years of living expenses = $100,000 ($50,000 per year).

Total Initial Savings requirement = $820,000 ($600K+$120K+$100K).

We are also providing a link to a Google Sheet where you can alter and play with different starting amounts, growth rate, and/or starting SS benefit amount.

Link for: GoogleSheet for Early Retiree Calculations

The user can change the fields highlighted in "Light Yellow" and put their own numbers, and see the change in results.

Table-1: Earnings and Withdrawals from Age 55-80, at 8.5% annual growth

Note: The living expenses are adjusted for inflation at 3% annually. The Social Security benefits are adjusted at 2.5% (for COLA, cost-of-living adjustment), but only after they start receiving them. The annualized growth rate of 8.5% (on a consistent basis) is taken, which is somewhat lower than the market's long-term returns.

Below, we will lay out an investment portfolio that should suit a large number of folks who are planning for a portfolio prior to and during retirement. Obviously, not everyone will find it suitable for their individual needs or temperament. Nonetheless, we only try to provide a framework that can easily be modified or adjusted to suit specific needs.

Our portfolio should have four basic goals or objectives:

  • Moderate to High Income (6% average)
  • Decent Growth of the Capital
  • Relative Safety of the Capital
  • Easy Maintenance and Upkeep.

Portfolio Structure:

We will suggest a bucket-based portfolio with three components (or buckets):

  • A Buy-and-Hold Bucket of Funds for High Income – 40% Allocation.
  • A Buy-and-Hold DGI Stocks bucket for Growth and Moderate Income – 35% allocation.
  • An Asset-Diversified Hedging Bucket – 25% Allocation.

The first two buckets are of a "buy-and-hold" nature and constitute 75% of the total capital. The buy-and-hold approach will ensure hassle-free, easy upkeep of the portfolio. In addition, these two buckets will also form the "core" investments of this portfolio. As stated above, the primary focus of the first bucket will be high (or moderate to high) income, while the second bucket will aim for market-matching growth with low to moderate income. The second bucket will consist of DGI stocks of companies that are large with stable sources of revenue, and also have substantial moats around their businesses.

The primary focus of the third bucket is to ensure the safety (on a relative basis) of the capital. While doing so, this bucket will also aim for high growth, at times exceeding the S&P 500. This bucket will also provide exposure to many different asset classes to ensure that we have a well-rounded overall portfolio. The income will be limited from this bucket.

Even though we are calling this third bucket a "Hedging" bucket, it involves no hedging cost as such. We consider it important to avoid the ongoing cost of hedging, as that would cut into total returns and add up over time. The best hedging method (without cost) that we know of is some form of a Rotational method. Such methods invariably involve monthly rotation (or trading), so it should preferably be done inside a tax-deferred account. Our goals for this bucket are market-matching or market-exceeding growth, while keeping the volatility and drawdowns low (at least 25-35% lower than the market).

(I) Bucket-1: A Bucket of Funds - 40% Allocation

We select seven funds (a combination of some ETFs and CEFs). The primary goal is to earn a high income but also to get decent growth over the long term. These seven funds have been selected from many different asset classes and/or sectors. The added advantage with funds is that they provide instant diversification.

The seven funds selected by us provide an average of 7.86% income yield, assuming that they have equal allocation.

There are seven funds that we selected: (SCHD), (ADX), (JEPQ), (PEO), (FAGIX), (UTG), and (BIZD).

(II) Bucket-2: A Bucket of Individual DGI Stocks - 35% Allocation

So, why are we having a DGI bucket to start with? Well, the funds bucket provided us with wide diversification and high income (nearly 8%). The aim of the DGI bucket is to hold some of the large-cap, blue-chip stocks with significant growth prospects or a wide moat around them. Some of the stocks we select will be focused on future growth with very minimal to low income yield. However, we also include some stocks that pay a reasonably high income yield consistently. Overall, the bucket will provide an average of 4.5% income yield.

We have selected 15 individual stocks. Individual investors could definitely change or adjust based on their suitability. The idea here is to provide a template for a balanced portfolio to meet the goals of yield, growth, and safety.

These stocks/securities are: (ADP), (LYB), (BTI), (KO), (LMT), (EPD), (COF), (ARCC), (CI), (MAA), (CNI), (UPS), (NNN), (AVGO), and (MSFT).

*Note: EPD is a partnership and provides partnership income instead of dividends. Please be aware of the tax treatment.

In spite of an income that is nearly 4 times that of the S&P 500, the DGI bucket outperformed the S&P 500 in the last 14 years (based on back testing).

((III)) Bucket-3: Hedging Bucket (Rotation Bucket) – 25% Allocation

Rotation Method: The bucket is based on SIX securities, but only two are selected every month for investment. At the end of each month, for each of the six securities, we will calculate the total performance returns (including any dividends/ distributions) over the previous three months. We will select the top TWO performing securities and invest 50% each in them for the next month. If the performance of the top two securities is lower than the risk-free return provided by Cash (or 1-3 months Treasury), then it would be replaced by cash for the next month. This process is repeated at the end of each month.

The six securities are:

  • (GLD) SPDR Gold Shares ETF.
  • (QQQ) Invesco QQQ Trust ETF.
  • (RQI) Cohen & Steers Qty Inc Realty.
  • (FAGIX) Fidelity Capital & Income Fund.
  • (TLT) iShares 20+ Year Treasury Bond ETF.
  • (VAW) Vanguard Materials Index ETF.

The Rotation method will provide three distinct benefits as demonstrated by the table below.

  • Market-exceeding (or at least market-matching) total returns.
  • Lower volatility.
  • Much lower drawdowns.

Risks:

There are always risks with any stock investments. The portfolio described in this article has a high concentration of equity investments. The element of bonds or fixed-income securities is relatively low. Even though the portfolio employs a hedging strategy in the form of a Rotational bucket, the allocation is only 25% and any hedging mechanism has limitations. Another risk comes from the duplication of some stocks, since the portfolio employs both a fund bucket and an individual-stocks bucket. However, overall duplication should be minor. If we were to enter into a prolonged correction, this portfolio would lose value, and even the income may be impacted to some extent.

Concluding Thoughts:

Early retirement is not for everyone. In fact, many folks find retirement boring and difficult to adjust to, at least in the first couple of years. On the flip side, at least a sizable minority would like to retire early if they could afford to. In this article, we are not talking about retiring in your 40s, but only about 10 years earlier than the regular retirement age. Nonetheless, as stated earlier, it requires prudent planning of your income versus expenses and substantial retirement capital to provide enough income and growth for many decades. The early retiree also needs to have a plan for how they are going to pursue their early retirement dreams, whatever they may be.

The bucket-based portfolio presented above should provide investors not only with a high income and high growth but also with some protection against tough market conditions, such as deep corrections and recessions.

The "Triple-Factor" Closed-End Fund Report

(This was formerly the "High-High-Low" CEF Report, now renamed to better reflect the systematic screening for three key attributes: high yield, strong coverage, and discount to NAV).

Our systematic approach screens over 400 closed-end funds to identify candidates meeting three critical criteria that balance yield, distribution sustainability, and value. This screening methodology originated from a member's suggestion:

Stanford, you should do a follow up article on CEF that distribute 8%+, have 90% or more coverage and trade under NAV. What does that look like in today's world? How many funds are doing that? What are the Z scores? I think many investors would be interested who are pushing for higher yield to identify best in class of aggressive funds that are coming close to covering dividends. Allows people to reevaluate risk/reward.

Therefore, the Triple-Factor screen identifies funds with the combination of:

  1. Substantial yield (>6.5%)
  2. Strong distribution coverage (>90%)
  3. Value opportunity (trading at a discount to NAV)

How is coverage calculated?

For the following fund houses, the latest trailing coverage numbers from the fund's latest unaudited monthly estimates are used:

  • PIMCO: trailing 6-month coverage
  • BlackRock: trailing 3-month coverage
  • Nuveen: trailing 3-month coverage
  • Eaton Vance: trailing 3-month coverage

Coverage ratios for the remaining funds are based on earnings data from their latest annual or semi-annual reports. Since these reports are published just twice yearly, these coverage figures may lag by up to 6 months compared to funds that provide monthly earnings estimates.

I hope that these rankings of Triple-Factor CEFs will provide fertile ground for further exploration and due diligence. As a reminder, these top lists are meant to be preliminary shortlists for further research, and are not meant to be the final analysis for a buy or sell decision.

P/D = premium/discount

data.title: Sandvik: Staying Neutral Until Revenue Growth Inflects
data.description:
Investment Action

I had a hold rating for Sandvik (OTCPK:SDVKY)(OTCPK:SDVKF) previously, as I thought the valuation was not cheap. While I saw a 40% upside over 4 years, that equates to only 10%/year, which I thought was not attractive. I was obviously wrong, as the share price went up ~15% in just ~3 months. The Q3 2025 results showed solid order momentum, notable recovery in MIM, and MRS saw good growth as it benefited from strong metals pricing. However, despite my higher conviction in management’s long-term guidance, valuation now looks full after the recent rally. At around 20x forward PE, near-term upside appears limited unless order strength quickly translates into revenue acceleration.

Q3 2025 Earnings Showed Strength

SDVKY 3Q25 results showed solid underlying demand, though reported figures were weighed down by FX effects and slower revenue conversion. Total orders grew 7% y/y to SEK 30.8 billion on a reported basis, but adjusted for FX, organic growth was 16%. Revenue came in at SEK 29.2 billion, down 4% as reported but up 5% organically, primarily due to timing delays between order intake and delivery. By segment, Mining and Rock Solutions [MRS] saw organic order intake up 24%. Even excluding the SEK 1.6 billion in major orders, organic growth was still 16%. Machining and Intelligent Manufacturing [MIM] saw 8% organic order growth, while Rock Processing [RP] saw 9% organic growth (or 14% excluding major orders).

While order growth was solid, profitability was weak, mainly due to external factors. SDVKY reported adj. EBITA of SEK 5.5 billion, down 6% y/y, with margins down 40 bps to 19%, and this was mostly due to SEK 837 million in FX headwinds that diluted margin by 130 bps. This resulted in an adj. profit of SEK 3.5 billion (EPS SEK 2.81, down 4% y/y).

Order Intake Signals Accelerating Growth

As you can see above, the clear highlight for SDVKY this quarter was the strength of its order intake. The 16% organic order growth significantly outpaced the 5% organic revenue growth by a full 1100 bps, which basically confirms that underlying demand remains solid. In fact, it has gotten better since this was an acceleration from 1H25. This order strength pushed the book-to-bill ratio to 105%, marking the third straight quarter above 100%, suggesting continued backlog growth, which gives better revenue visibility and meaningfully reduces near-term growth risk. This order momentum did not stop in Q3. Management commentary implied this order momentum has persisted into the Q4 quarter. Specifically, they commented that daily order intake for the first two weeks of October was stable relative to Q3 levels, after adjusting for normal seasonality. This is very encouraging because it suggests that underlying demand is not a one-off blip.

This sets up very well for FY2026, as orders received today would translate into revenue growth in the coming quarters. With momentum being strong in Q3 and even Q4, there is a good chance FY2026 will see revenue growth acceleration (remember organic growth in Q3 was only 5%).

MRS Segment

The core driver for SDVKY’s equity story is its MRS segment, as I mentioned before, and it is great to learn that it is showing accelerating momentum. The 24% y/y organic order growth was great, but even better if you consider that equipment orders grew 75% organically and that brownfield projects came in at ~70% of equipment orders vs. the usual 50%, which I believe reflects genuine pent-up demand from miners reinvesting after a period of under-investing.

The consequential impact from an accelerating equipment growth is that it extends the customer lifetime value as SDVKY benefits from more aftermarket sales opportunities. And as expected, the aftermarket business continues to be robust (still at low 70% of total MRS revenue) with double-digit organic order growth in Parts and Services.

You might ask what is really driving the sudden surge in equipment orders here. I see a few reasons for this. Firstly, crucial metals continue to fetch high prices, especially gold and copper, which were explicitly called out by management as very strong drivers in Q3 and were ~60% of the MRS business. High prices naturally incentivize miners to act. Secondly, brownfield is the natural choice for miners because it is faster to ramp it up vs. greenfield. And because of this “shorter cycle,” SDVKY is able to see a surge in equipment orders (for greenfield, because of the timeline, equipment orders come later). Thirdly, it links back to what I said before. There is an aging global mining fleet that needs replacement, and given the pricing environment, it makes sense for miners to commit and upgrade their equipment.

Very importantly, this accelerating equipment sales cycle heavily improves customer lifetime value [CLTV]. Remember that each new piece of machinery (particularly those featuring SDVKY’s AutoMine and digital automation platforms) makes the relationship stickier between SDVKY and the customer. Once deployed, these technologies become mission-critical for a mine’s daily operations and also generate performance data for analytics and tracking purposes. As such, these machines require ongoing software updates to make sure they are working properly to ensure full productivity. This dynamic makes miners highly reliant on SDVKY’s continuous “service” and software ecosystem to operate effectively, and all these significantly increase switching costs.

As you can imagine, every new sale then enhances the CLTV because of the higher-margin aftermarket activity. This dynamic is already visible. The aftermarket business maintained strong momentum in 3Q25, delivering double-digit organic order growth in parts and services.

Machining Recovery

Another very encouraging development in Q3 2025 was the recovery in MIM. After several quarters of poor demand that had dampened the stock sentiment, finally, MIM delivered 8% organic order growth, which marked an inflection point. Importantly, this rebound was broad-based rather than dependent on one-off tailwinds or pricing effects. The core part of this business (cutting tools) saw high-single-digit organic order growth y/y, and management noted that demand remained stable sequentially, with a strong finish in September. This gives confidence that the trough may have passed, and Q4 may continue to see this momentum. Aside from the cutting tool, the powder business also performed well, delivering low double-digit growth, which I expect to continue because I believe tungsten prices will stay elevated for the foreseeable future due to ongoing supply constraints.

On an overall basis, management did also mention that daily orders in early October were stable vs. Q3 2025 levels, which suggests that this recovery trend could be a real start of a growth inflection. The rebound in MIM is very important for the business because it diversifies SDVKY beyond just mining. As industrial production normalizes eventually, the MIM segment should see strong recovery. The reason is straightforward. The core products in this MIM segment are metal-cutting tools, and these are consumables used across multiple industries (ranging from automotive and aerospace to general engineering). When factory utilization increases, the machines run longer, and that naturally drives up consumption and replacement demand for these tools.

My sense is that we are inching towards an inflection here because management commented on stabilization in general engineering demand during Q3 2025. If this industrial cycle continues to move in the right direction, MIM should see revenue growth acceleration in the coming quarters.

Valuation

I am not changing my long estimates for SDVKY, as I still think management's long-term guidance is a good anchor to get a sense of the upside. My conviction of SDVKY meeting this target has certainly gone up, but given the share price increase so far, I am still not so sure if I want to give this a buy rating. Even after adjusting my multiple expectations to 20x forward PE on FY30 estimates (which is already a premium to peers that have a similar revenue growth outlook of high single digits on average), I get to 11% IRR (or 36% upside over 3 years). To be fair, 11% IRR is not bad for most retail investors, but I am seeking >15% IRR opportunities.

Hence, I am still going to stick to my hold rating. But for those that want to give this a shot. I see a scenario where near-term IRR could be higher than 11%. If SDVKY is able to show real inflection in revenue growth (translated from the current strong order book growth), the market may very well push multiples higher against the backdrop of faster earnings growth in the near term. In this case, we could see another round of >15% upside in just a few months (like it did recently).

Risk

The near-term risk is a significant FX headwind, which could lead to poorer than expected reported results. There are also internal execution risks. The Mining ERP transition execution has not been as great (residual costs from this impacted Q3 2025 margins by 30 bps), and should this not be fixed, we could see this linger into Q4 2025. Furthermore, converting the strong order book into timely revenue remains key. This conversion may not happen in Q4, and that could weigh on sentiment.

Conclusion

I maintain a hold rating on SDVKY. The order momentum across MRS and MIM is very solid, and its aftermarket mix makes the long-term growth outlook better. However, order conversion timing and valuation make me stay cautious. I would turn more bullish if stronger revenue translation and margin recovery emerge in the coming quarters. For now, I agree SDVKY is a solid long-term name to monitor, but I prefer to wait for a clearer inflection or a more attractive re-entry level.
